coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent sensor for detecting dopamine and tyrosinase activity by dual-emission carbon dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Fei
Huang, Wei
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a fluorescence strategy for detecting dopamine (DA) and sensing tyrosinase (TYR) activity on the basis of the dual-emission carbon dots (DECDs), which contain two emitters: the blue emitters (BE, maximum emission at 385nm) and yellow emitters (YE, maximum emission at 530nm). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) can effectively quench the two emissions of DECDs. The addition of DA aggregates AuNPs effectively, leading to the fluorescence recovery of dual emitters gradually. This strategy exhibits a high selectivity toward DA and shows good linear ranges, such as 0.5–3μM for BE and 0.1–3μM for YE. Additionally, the proposed method is successfully applied to the determination of DA in real samples with satisfactory recoveries. Subsequently, this DECDs-AuNPs platform is further taken advantage to assess TYR activity by the aid of TYR’s capability for oxidation of DA into dopaquinone, which will not induce the agglomeration of AuNPs, so the fluorescence quenching of DECDs is associated with TYR activity. Finally, the mechanism of the reaction is discussed in detail, and the results suggest that both amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups of DA bring the aggregation of AuNPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Dopamine
Tyrosinase activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The interaction between DBA and DA
Detection of DA in human plasma
The interaction between DECDs and AuNPs
Fluorescence recovery of DECDs-AuNPs system by DA
Optimizing experimental conditions of DA detection
The detection of TYR on the basis of DECDs-AuNPs-DA system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the detection strategy for DA and TYR activity based on aggregation and disaggregation of AuNPs.
Fluorescence spectra (a, BE; c, YE) of DECDs upon addition of different concentrations of AuNPs and the quenching efficiency of DECDs in the presence of different volumes of AuNPs (b, BE; d, YE).
Fluorescence spectra of BE (a) and YE (b) in absence and presence of DA, AuNPs, and the complex of DA with AuNPs.
Fluorescence spectra of DECDs-AuNPs system in the presence of different concentrations of DA (a, BE; c, YE) and corresponding linear ranges (b, BE; d, YE).

Selectivity of DECDs-AuNPs system for the detection of DA over glucose, tryptophan, heparin, epinephrine, L-phenyylalanine, catechol, p-nitrophenol, levodopa and ascorbic acid (a, BE; b, YE). For the dual emissions, the concentration of DA is 2μM, and all of the interferences are 20μM.
Fluorescence spectra of free DECDs (line 1) and DECDs in the presence of TYR (line 2), AuNPs (line 3), both AuNPs and DA (line 4), and the complex of DA, TYR and AuNPs (line 5).

Fluorescence spectra of the mixture containing DECDs, AuNPs and DA in the presence of varying amounts of TYR (a, BE; c, YE). Linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity of dual emissions and concentration of TYR (b, BE; d, YE).

(a) UV-absorption spectra of free AuNPs (curve 1) and AuNPs in the presence of DA (curve 2), catechol (curve 3), TYR (curve 4) and both DA and TYR (curve 5); (b) the corresponding photographs according to the curves in Fig. 7a and the structures of DA, catechol and dopaquinone; (c) TEM image of AuNPs in the presence of DA.

Fluorescence spectra of the mixture containing DECDs, AuNPs and DA in the presence of varying amounts of DBA (a, BE; b, YE). The linear range of DBA is from 1 to 200nM for BE, and for YE, a good linear range is observed from 1 to 500nM.
Determination of DA in human plasma.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent sensor for detecting dopamine and tyrosinase activity by dual-emission carbon dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Intermediates and Analysis of Natural Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Xining 810001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, Shandong, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Fei Qu and Wei Huang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a fluorescence strategy for detecting dopamine (DA) and sensing tyrosinase (TYR) activity on the basis of the dual-emission carbon dots (DECDs), which contain two emitters: the blue emitters (BE, maximum emission at 385nm) and yellow emitters (YE, maximum emission at 530nm). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) can effectively quench the two emissions of DECDs. The addition of DA aggregates AuNPs effectively, leading to the fluorescence recovery of dual emitters gradually. This strategy exhibits a high selectivity toward DA and shows good linear ranges, such as 0.5–3μM for BE and 0.1–3μM for YE. Additionally, the proposed method is successfully applied to the determination of DA in real samples with satisfactory recoveries. Subsequently, this DECDs-AuNPs platform is further taken advantage to assess TYR activity by the aid of TYR’s capability for oxidation of DA into dopaquinone, which will not induce the agglomeration of AuNPs, so the fluorescence quenching of DECDs is associated with TYR activity. Finally, the mechanism of the reaction is discussed in detail, and the results suggest that both amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups of DA bring the aggregation of AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (3, 4-dihydroxyphenylethylamine, DA), a catecholamine neurotransmitter, is ubiquitous in a variety of animals with a series of functions in the central nervous system [1]. The disorder levels of DA are considered to be the indicator of some neurological syndromes, such as schizophrenia [2] and Parkinson's disease [3]. Tyrosinase (TYR), which can catalyze DA to dopaquinone [4], is a copper-containing polyphenol oxidase and widespread in nature [5]. In the human body, an abnormally elevated level of TYR is associated with several diseases, including melanoma cancer and vitiligo [6]. Therefore, there is an urgent demand for the development of simple, selective, and sensitive assays for DA and TYR activity.

Up until now, a large variety of analytical methods have been employed to determine DA and TYR successfully including electrochemical analysis [7,8] and the fluorescence method [9–13]. Although the sensitivity of electrochemical methods is satisfactory, the repeatability and the anti-interference ability of these sensors should be further improved. While the fluorescence detection of DA is usually established by the direct interaction between DA and nano-probes, for instance, DA could effectively quench the emissions of β-cyclodextrin functionalized gold nanoclusters [9], adenosine capped CdSe/ZnS quantum dots [10] and graphene quantum dots [11]. Furthermore, the detection of TYR is always based on the catalyze reaction between DA and TYR. For example, when DA was transformed to dopaquinone under the catalysis of TYR, the dopaquinone could quench the fluorescence of the gold/silver nanoclusters [12] and glutathione protected gold nanoclusters [13]. Consequently, the fluorescence change was directly correlated to the concentration of TYR by taking advantage of the different effect of DA and dopaquinone.

Recently, our group has developed a green and simple synthesis of dual-emission carbon dots (DECDs), which contain two different emitters: the blue emitters (BE, λex=315nm, λem=386nm) and the yellow emitters (YE, λex=365nm, λem=530nm) [14]. The fluorescence of dual emitters can be quenched effectively by gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) within 5min. After addition of DA, the aggregation of AuNPs occurs, causing the dual emissions restore rapidly. The mechanism study reveals that both the amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups of DA together bring the aggregation of AuNPs. Hence, when DA is oxidized to dopaquinone by TYR, AuNPs keep the dispersed state rather than agglomeration, and the fluorescence of two emitters is quenched again. Importantly, this DECDs-AuNPs system paves a new way to detect DA and TYR simultaneously, and the determination is simple, rapid and convenient. The limits of detection (LOD) are 0.037μM and 0.0098U/mL for DA and TYR, respectively. Benefiting from the superior sensitivity and selectivity, this DECDs-AuNPs system is successfully applied for the detection of DA in human plasma samples.

Ascorbic acid, ethylene glycol, sodium citrate, dopamine hydrochloride, tyrosinase from mushroom, chloroauric acid, glucose, L-tryptophan, heparin, p-nitrophenol, hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Levodopa, epinephrine, L-phenylalanine and catechol were purchased from Xiya Reagent (Shandong, China). The 58-mer dopamine-binding aptamer (DBA) with the sequence of 5′- GTC TCT GTG TGC GCC AGA GAA CAC TGG GGC AGA TAT GGG CCA GCA CAG AAT GAG GCC C-3′ was designed by the reported study [15] and synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification. Deionized water (18MΩcm) was used throughout the experiments.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were performed on a Cary 300 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100PLUS (JEOL, Japan).

In this paper, DECDs were synthesized according to our previous work [14]. The procedure was briefly described as follows: 0.8g activated AA was dissolved with deionized water (10mL), and then the above solution was stirred thoroughly to form a homogeneous solution after the introduction of concentrated ethylene glycol (10mL). Subsequently, this mixture was transferred into an autoclave reactor and heated at 160°C for 70min, and then it was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 10min. Finally, a clear yellow aqueous dispersion containing DECDs was obtained and stored at ambient environment.

The AuNPs were prepared by the citrate reduction method according to previous report [16]. Firstly, all the glass wares were washed with aqua regia (mixture of HCl and HNO3 at a 3:1 ratio) and then with deionized water. Secondly, the HAuCl4 solution (95mL H2O, 0.01g HAuCl4) was heated to boil under stirring. Next, the sodium citrate solution (5mL, 0.01%(w/v)) was added rapidly. Then the solution was boiled for another 15min with the color of solution changing from pale yellow to wine red. At last, the solution was cooled to room temperature with continuous stirring and stored in refrigerator at 4°C.

The fluorescence detection of DA was performed as follows: for BE, a fixed amount (200μL) of AuNPs solution was incubated with different concentrations of DA for 10min under the condition of pH 6.0 (20mM PB buffer); subsequently, 11μL (10μL/mL) of DECDs solution was added to the mixture and allowed to equilibrate for 5min; then the fluorescence of resultant solution was recorded at 386nm (emission) with the excitation wavelength of 315nm. For YE, the different concentrations of DA and AuNPs solutions (200μL) were incubated for 10min in PB buffer (pH 6.3, 20mM); next, 90μL of DECDs solution (30μL/mL) was added; following another 5min of incubation, the fluorescence of the mixture was measured at 530nm with an excitation of 365nm.

The concentration of DA was linearly response to the fluorescence difference F-F0 (F0 was the original fluorescence intensity of DECDs-AuNPs system, and F was the fluorescence intensity of DECDs-AuNPs system with the addition of various concentrations of DA). All data were the average values obtained by three parallel experiments at least in this paper.

Typically, at first, for BE, different levels of TYR ranging from 0.05 to 1U/mL were respectively added into the mixture of DA solution (2μM) and PB solution (pH 6.0, 20mM) for incubation of 30min; then, 200μL AuNPs and 11μL DECDs (10μL/mL) were added with vigorous stirring. For YE, the diverse concentrations of TYR and 2μM of DA solutions were added into a 1.5mL of epoxide (EP) tube and followed by the introduction of PB solution (pH 6.3, 20mM); the mixtures were incubated for 30min; after that, 200μL AuNPs and 90μL (30μL/mL) of the diluted YE were added to EP tube. The volume of final solution was 1000μL. The fluorescence intensity decreased linearly with increasing the concentration of TYR, and the change of fluorescence intensity was expressed as F-F1, in which F and F1 represented the fluorescence intensities of DECDs-AuNPs-DA system in the absence and presence of TYR, respectively.

Besides, LOD is the limit of detection, which is defined as the concentration of analyte that corresponds to three times the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N=3) [17]. LOD is calculated according to the expression LOD=3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution (n=10), and K is the slope of the calibration curve. The limit of quantification (LOQ) is the minimum amount that can be quantitatively measured by the instrument. In the fluorescence detection, the LOQ is the lowest concentration on the standard curve that can be measured with acceptable accuracy [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between DBA and DA
ce:section.ce:para: The DBA was heated to 95°C for 5min (to dissociate any intermolecular interaction) and then rapidly cooled in ice for 20min. For BE, the diverse concentrations of DBA were respectively mixed with DA solution (2μM) and 20mM PB solution (pH 6.0) for 1h under vortex mixing to make sure that DA and DBA interacted with each other sufficiently; then the AuNPs solutions (200μL) were mixed together; subsequently, the DECDs 11μL (10μL/mL) were added with vigorous stirring. For YE, the appropriate concentration of DA (2μM) and 20mM PB solution (pH 6.3) were added to the different concentrations of DBA with vigorous stirring, and the mixture was incubated for 1h; successively, the AuNPs solutions (200μL) were introduced to the above mentioned solution and homogenized by vigorous stirring; eventually, the DECDs solution 90μL (30μL/mL) were added. The fluorescence spectra of these samples were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of DA was carried out in deproteinized human plasma. The plasma proteins were precipitated using acetonitrile (mixture of human plasma and acetonitrile at a 1:2 ratio) by centrifugation at 10000rpm for 20min [19]. The supernatant was collected and 100 times diluted with deionized water before use. Different concentrations of DA were spiked into the deproteinized blank human plasma and quantified by the present sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction between DECDs and AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to this work, our group has successfully studied the synthesis and characterization of DECDs, which have been constructed as various biosensors [20–22]. The fluorescence spectra indicate that the DECDs contain two different emitters: The BE can exhibit blue fluorescence emission band centered at 386nm with the excitation wavelength of 315nm, and the yellow fluorescence of YE is obtained at 530nm when excitation is 365nm (Fig. S1). These dual emissions can maintain stable for at least 1 month at room temperature. The DECDs have a relative narrow size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 2.5nm with an average diameter of about 2.08±0.54nm, which is calculated by averaging the size of 100 dots randomly in TEM image (Fig. S2).

The AuNPs are synthesized by the citrate reduction method [16], and highly dispersed in solution. These nanoparticles show wine-red color with a surface plasmon resonance peak at 520nm [23] (Fig. S3a). Besides, AuNPs have good round shape and uniform size with the average diameter of 18.01±0.45nm which was determined by averaging the size of 50 particles from the analysis of TEM image (Fig. S3b).

The fluorescence of DECDs can be gradually quenched by AuNPs with increasing the volume of AuNPs (Fig. 1 ). When the volume of AuNPs is 200μL, the fluorescence of two emitters is quenched to 60% approximately. While excessive AuNPs are disadvantage for the sensitive detection of DA, 200μL AuNPs are chosen for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence recovery of DECDs-AuNPs system by DA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescent spectra of DECDs (line 1) is identical to that of the mixture of DECDs and DA (line 2), indicating that there is no interaction between DA and DECDs; when DECDs are mixed with AuNPs (line 3), the two emissions are quenched effectively; however, with addition of DA, the fluorescence intensities of DECDs enhance obviously (line 4), suggesting that the interaction between DA and AuNPs can produce the fluorescence recovery of DECDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing experimental conditions of DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction conditions are investigated in this assay. The optimum concentration of BE is 0.11μL/mL, and for YE, the suitable concentration is 2.7μL/mL (Fig. S4). While AuNPs can quench the dual emissions within 5min, with addition of DA, the fluorescence recoveries of the two emitters reach the maximum values after 10min, suggesting a fast reaction rate among DECDs, AuNPs and DA (Fig. S5 and S6). Besides, the fluorescence of DECDs can maintain stable over the pH range of 3–8 [14], thus PB buffers are selected to explore the influence of pH in the detection of DA, and the ideal pH values for BE and YE are 6.0 and 6.3, respectively (Fig. S7). The temperature is another key factor in the reaction, and higher temperature ( >30°C) is found to hinder the reaction among DECDs, AuNPs, and DA, so 25°C is chosen in the following experiment (Fig. S8 and S9).

Under the optimized conditions, at the excitation of 315nm (Fig. 3 a and b) the fluorescence intensity of DECDs–AuNPs system is sensitive to DA, and rises with increasing concentrations of DA. There is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence recovery and DA concentration in the range from 0.5–3μM with LOD 0.23μM and LOQ is 0.5μM. Moreover, in terms of YE (Fig. 3c and d), with the enhancement of DA concentrations, the fluorescence intensity also enhances gradually, and a good linear range of DA is observed from 0.1 to 3μM with LOD as low as 0.037μM and LOQ is 0.1μM. On the other hand, for both the two emitters, there are two linear ranges for DA (two different slops), revealing that the fluorescence recovery contains two speeds in various linear ranges. The reason may be related to the different rate of aggregation of AuNPs induced by the increasing concentration of DA (Fig. S10).

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, some structurally similar derivatives, such as levodopa, epinephrine, L-phenylalanine, and catechol, and other compounds, including p-nitrophenol, L-tryptophan, heparin, ascorbic acid and glucose are tested. Compared with the response to 2μM DA, the fluorescence recoveries are lower significantly than DA in the presence of 10 times of the above-mentioned species. The results clearly demonstrate that both of the dual emitters have excellent selectivity for DA (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of TYR on the basis of DECDs-AuNPs-DA system
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 5 , there is no significant difference between the dual emissions and the fluorescence of DECDs-TYR compounds. DA can hind the quenching effect of AuNPs on DECDs. Subsequently, DA is oxidized to dopaquinone by TYR, and the emissions of DECDs are dramatically quenched again. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 6 the fluorescence change of dual emissions is proportional to the TYR concentration in the range from 0.05 to 1U/mL for BE (LOD 0.021U/mL and LOQ 0.05U/mL), and 0.025–1U/mL for YE (LOD 0.0098U/mL and LOQ 0.025U/mL). The linear ranges and LODs of this sensing system are also not inferior to other fluorescent sensors based nano-materials [24,25]. Besides, both of the dual emitters have two linear regions for TYR detection, illustrating that the fluorescence quenching has two different speeds, which are related to the different rate of oxidation of DA by TYR, and the detail discussion is displayed in Fig. S11 and S12 .

Herein, the interaction between DA and AuNPs recovers the fluorescence of DECDs, and the reason is ascribed to the aggregation of AuNPs induced by DA. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a (curve 1), AuNPs which highly dispersed in solution by charged citrate ions have a surface plasmon resonance peak at 520nm. Whereas DA is introduced, a new absorption peak at 687nm appears (Fig. 7a, curve 2) and the color of AuNPs changes rapidly from wine-red to blue (Fig. 7b). Moreover, TEM image also verifies that DA can aggregate AuNPs efficiently (Fig. 7c). Subsequently, some experiments are employed to explore the aggregate mechanism.
1.
Effect of amine group of DA on the aggregation of AuNPs.
Comparing with DA, catechol cannot cause the red shift of absorption band and the color change of AuNPs (Fig. 7a, curve 3), revealing that the lack of amine group will not cause the agglomeration of AuNPs.
2.
Effect of phenolic hydroxyl group of DA on the aggregation of AuNPs.
At first, TYR can not aggregate AuNPs (Fig. 7a, curve 4). Then DA is converted to dopaquinone by TYR, and the absorbance of AuNPs at 520nm rises, while the position of peak and the color of solution keep steady (Fig. 7a curve 5, and Fig. 7b). It implies that the hydroxyl groups of DA also contribute to AuNPs aggregating.
3.
Specific recognition of DBA to DA.
Dopamine-binding-aptamers (DBA) exhibits high specific recognition towards DA. Comparing with DBA’s random flexible structure, the conformation of DBA is changed to a rigid stem-loop structure with the formation of DBA-DA complex [26,27]. Therefore, with addition of DBA, DA is not exposed outside and AuNPs will disperse well, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of dual emissions again (Fig. 8 ). The fluorescence drops proportionately with increasing concentrations of target DBA. It suggests that the DA-induced aggregation of AuNPs is a key modulator for dual emissions.

Hence, we speculate that the agglomeration of AuNPs caused by DA may involve two steps: (1) DA tends to readily adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs due to the chemisorptions-type interactions by the amine group; (2) the H-bonding interaction is initiated between neighboring phenolic hydroxyl groups of the neighboring DA, leading to drawing the distance of AuNPs and aggregating (Scheme 1 ). Finally, the dispersed or aggregated state of AuNPs modulates the pattern of fluorescence “turn-off” or “turn-on”. The similar mechanism has been reported by several literatures [3,28].

The developed system is applied to the determination of DA in deproteinized human plasma samples. The results obtained by using the standard addition method are shown in Table 1 , and the satisfactory recoveries of DA in the real samples from 92% to 106% reveal the good reliability of this method.

In this paper, the dual emissions of DECDs can be quenched effectively by AuNPs, and then DA makes AuNPs aggregate, resulting in the fluorescence recovery. This method shows good linear ranges of DA and exhibits a high selectivity toward some structurally similar derivatives and other compounds. By virtue of this sensing platform, the detection of DA in plasma samples is realized successfully. On the other hand, TYR can catalyze the oxidation of DA into dopaquinone, which cannot induce the agglomeration of AuNPs, producing the fluorescence quenching of DECDs again, so this platform can detect the TYR’s activity sensitively. Furthermore, several experiments verify that the amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups of DA together bring the aggregation of AuNPs. Therefore, this DECDs-AuNPs system paves a new way to detect DA and TYR simultaneously, and also as a proper candidate explores the interaction between DA and DBA.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Youth Fund Project) (21405093) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University (BSQD20130117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2017.11.055.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
